intro.title: Quantifiers: some, any, much, many, all, etc.

sections.title: What are quantifiers?
sections.content:
**Quantifiers** tell us how much or how many. They come before nouns: **some** water, **many** books, **all** students.

Common quantifiers: **some, any, much, many, a lot of, lots of, all, most, no, none, few, a few, little, a little, enough, several, each, every.**

The choice depends on: **countable vs uncountable**, and **positive vs question/negative**.

sections.title: Some and any
sections.content:
**Some** – positive statements and offers/requests:
• I have some milk. There are some books. Would you like some tea?

**Any** – questions and negatives:
• Do you have any milk? I don't have any money. Is there any bread?

**Some in questions** when we expect 'yes' (offer): Would you like some cake? Can I have some water?

sections.title: Much and many
sections.content:
**Much** – uncountable nouns (often in questions/negatives):
• How much water? I don't have much time.

**Many** – countable nouns (often in questions/negatives):
• How many books? There aren't many people.

**In positive statements** we often use **a lot of** instead: I have a lot of time. (not: I have much time)

sections.title: A lot of, lots of, enough
sections.content:
**A lot of / lots of** – both countable and uncountable; positive:
• A lot of people. Lots of milk. She has a lot of friends.

**Enough** – sufficient; before noun: enough time, enough money; after adjective: old enough, big enough.
• We have enough water. She's old enough to drive.

sections.title: All, most, no, none
sections.content:
**All** – the whole amount/group: all the water, all students, all (of) the books.
**Most** – the majority: most people, most of the time.
**No** – zero, not any: no money, no problems. (No + noun = positive verb: I have no time = I don't have any time.)
**None** – not any (used without noun): None of it. None left.

sections.title: Few, a few, little, a little
sections.content:
**Few / a few** – with countable nouns:
• **Few** = not many (negative): Few people came. (almost nobody)
• **A few** = some (positive): I have a few friends. (a small number)

**Little / a little** – with uncountable nouns:
• **Little** = not much (negative): Little progress was made.
• **A little** = some (positive): I need a little help.

sections.title: Quick summary
sections.content:
• **Some** – positive; **any** – questions/negatives (some in offers).
• **Much** – uncountable; **many** – countable (often in questions/negatives).
• **A lot of** – both; positive statements.
• **Few** (negative) vs **a few** (positive); **little** (negative) vs **a little** (positive).
• **All, most, no, none** – whole/zero.

sections.title: Typical errors
sections.content:
• **Any in positive:** Wrong: I have *any* milk. Right: I have **some** milk.
• **Much with countable:** Wrong: *much* books. Right: **many** books.
• **Many with uncountable:** Wrong: *many* water. Right: **much** water.
• **Few vs a few:** Few people = almost nobody. A few people = some people.
• **Little vs a little:** Little hope = almost no hope. A little hope = some hope.

questions.question: 'I have _____ milk.' (positive) Which fits?
'How _____ books did you read?' Which fits?
'She has _____ friends.' (a small number, positive) Which fits?
'There isn't _____ time.' Which fits?
'I need _____ help.' (a small amount, positive) Which fits?
I have _____ milk in the fridge.
Do you have _____ sugar?
How _____ people were at the party?
She has _____ friends in London.
There is _____ water left. (almost none)
We have _____ time to finish. (some)
_____ the students passed the exam.
I don't have _____ money.
There are _____ apples on the table. (a small number)
He has _____ experience in teaching.
questions.explanation: Positive statement, uncountable → a lot of.

questions.question: We don't need to buy more bread. There's _____ bread in the cupboard. (more than sufficient)
questions.explanation: Plenty of = more than enough; uncountable, positive.

questions.question: I've had two cups already. I'd like _____ coffee, please. (additional)
Of all the options, this one causes _____ trouble. (minimum amount)
We have _____ time to catch the train. (sufficient)
I wanted some milk but there was _____ left. (not any)
questions.explanation: Not any (without noun) → none. 'There was none left.'

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have any milk.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'How much books did you read?' Correct the sentence.
answers: How many books did you read?
How many books did you read

questions.question: Wrong: 'She has few friends.' (meaning she has some friends) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'I need a little eggs.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'There isn't some bread.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'How many water do you need?' Correct the sentence.
answers: How much water do you need?
How much water do you need
